title: Performance — technique and expression
summary: This lesson deconstructs the essential relationship between technique and expression in IB Dance performance. It moves beyond the mere execution of steps to explore how a performer embodies artistic intention, manipulates dynamics, and achieves a compelling performance quality that meets the top bands of the HL assessment criteria.
learningObjectives: Analyse the symbiotic relationship between technical proficiency and expressive range in performance.
Articulate the artistic intention of a choreographed work and identify specific strategies to embody it.
Apply concepts of dynamic range, phrasing, and focus to refine performance quality.
Evaluate personal performance using the language of the IB Dance assessment criteria for Performance.
Develop strategies for consistent and confident performance under assessment conditions.

simpleExplanation.title: The Performer's Blueprint: Fusing Technique and Soul
simpleExplanation.summary: Exceptional dance performance isn't just about doing the right moves; it's about making those moves mean something. This involves mastering the physical 'how' (technique) so you can fully communicate the emotional and artistic 'why' (expression).
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a master calligrapher. Their technique is the precise control of the brush, the pressure, the angle, and the flow of ink. This technical mastery doesn't just create legible letters; it allows them to imbue the characters with grace, power, or delicacy. The technique is the vehicle for expression; without it, the artistic intention remains a smudge on the page.
steps: Deconstruct the choreography's artistic intention: Before focusing on steps, analyse the 'why'. What is the core idea, emotion, or narrative? Write it down.
Isolate and refine technical elements: Drill specific movements—a turn, a floor transition, a gesture—focusing on clarity, control, and efficiency. Film yourself to check alignment and form.

Layer expressive qualities: Rehearse the same phrase multiple times, experimenting with dynamics (sharp vs. sustained), focus (internal vs. external), and phrasing (breathing with the music vs. moving against it).

Integrate and rehearse for consistency: Combine the refined technique and expressive choices. Practise performing the entire piece to build stamina and ensure your communication of intent is clear and sustained from start to finish.

flashcards.back: The physical skills and competencies required to execute dance movements with clarity, control, and efficiency. It encompasses alignment, balance, coordination, strength, and flexibility.
The articulation of thoughts, emotions, and artistic ideas through movement. It involves the use of dynamics, focus, phrasing, and projection to communicate meaning to an audience.
The underlying purpose, theme, or concept of a dance work as envisioned by the choreographer. The performer's primary role is to interpret and communicate this intention.
The spectrum of energy or force applied to movement, from sharp and percussive to soft and sustained. A wide dynamic range is a hallmark of a sophisticated performance.
An assessment criterion term referring to the precision and definition of body shapes, lines, and pathways in space. It demonstrates a high level of technical control.
The ability of a performer to radiate energy and intention to fill the performance space and engage the audience. It is both an internal state of commitment and an external communication.
The sensory perception of one's own body in space—its position, movement, and orientation. It is crucial for coordination, balance, and partner work.
A holistic term describing the overall impact and polish of a performance. It combines technical proficiency, expressive depth, and confident presentation.
The direction of the performer's gaze and internal concentration. Focus can be used to direct the audience's attention, establish mood, and reveal character or intention.
The ability to hear, interpret, and embody the qualities of the accompaniment. It goes beyond rhythm to include melody, texture, and dynamics.
The shaping of movement sequences with a clear beginning, middle, and end, often linked to breath or musical phrases. It gives movement a sense of grammar and flow.
The deep, physical integration of an idea or quality, where the movement is not just a representation but a manifestation of the artistic intention. The dancer 'becomes' the idea.
The movements that connect one shape or phrase to another. Seamless and intentional transitions are a key indicator of high-level technical and performance skill.

faq.q: What if I make a mistake during my assessed performance?
faq.a: Examiners are experienced and understand that live performance carries risks. A minor stumble or memory lapse will not automatically result in a low mark. What is being assessed is your ability to recover. A confident performer will acknowledge the error internally, immediately refocus, and integrate back into the choreography seamlessly. Panicking or stopping will draw more attention to the mistake. Your ability to maintain your focus and commitment to the performance's intention despite a mistake is a high-level skill.

faq.q: How can I show 'expression' if the piece is very abstract?
faq.a: Expression is not limited to narrative or emotion. In an abstract piece, expression relates to the communication of concepts like space, time, and energy. You can express 'fragmentation' through sharp, disconnected movements, or 'flow' through sustained, fluid phrases. Your focus, dynamic choices, and relationship to the space all contribute to expressing the work's abstract intention. It's about embodying the *quality* of the movement, not a specific character or story.

faq.q: Do I need to be extremely flexible or have perfect turnout to get high marks for technique?
faq.a: While flexibility and turnout are components of certain dance genres, IB Dance assesses technique more holistically. Examiners are looking for clarity, control, coordination, and efficiency *within your own physical capabilities*. It is better to execute a 90-degree extension with perfect control and a clear line than to struggle to hold a 120-degree extension with poor alignment and visible tension. The assessment values the quality and intelligence of your movement over extreme physical feats. Work to improve your physical range, but prioritise control and clarity in your performance.

faq.q: How is my solo performance weighted against my group performances?
faq.a: For the Performance component, you present two to three pieces totalling 6-9 minutes. At least one must be a solo, and at least one must be a group piece. Your final mark is a holistic judgement based on your performance across all pieces. The assessor looks for your skills in different contexts. Your solo showcases individual artistry and responsibility, while group work demonstrates skills of timing, spatial awareness, and collaboration. You should aim to demonstrate excellence in both contexts.

sections.content: In the IB Dance HL course, the Performance component assesses your ability to function as an interpreter and communicator of dance. This is not a simple test of physical prowess. Examiners are looking for a sophisticated integration of technical skill and expressive communication. A performance that achieves the highest marks is one where the technique is so well-mastered that it becomes invisible, serving only to illuminate the artistic intention of the work. This lesson will guide you in cultivating that seamless fusion.

At the HL level, 'technique' transcends simply 'getting the steps right'. It is the dancer's vocabulary. A limited vocabulary restricts what you can say; a rich and nuanced vocabulary allows for poetry. In dance, this means your technique must be secure enough to allow for subtlety and risk-taking. Examiners assess technique through 'clarity of form and line', 'control', and 'coordination'.

items: **Clarity of Form:** Focus on the precision of your body shapes. In an arabesque, is the line from fingertip to toe clear and unbroken? In floorwork, are the shapes of the torso and limbs deliberate and well-defined?

**Control:** This is evident in balances, turns, and especially in transitions. Can you sustain a balance without visible tension? Can you move from a fast, dynamic phrase into a moment of stillness with absolute control?

**Efficiency:** Advanced technique is not about brute force, but about using the minimum energy required for maximum effect. This involves correct alignment, use of breath to initiate movement, and understanding principles like release and suspension.
**Consistency:** A top-band performer demonstrates technical proficiency throughout the entire piece, not just in isolated moments. This requires significant stamina and concentration.

sections.question: Analyse how you would approach refining the technical aspects of a slow, adagio-style solo to meet the 'Clarity of Form' criterion.
sections.solution: In my reflection, I would first identify the key shapes and lines central to the adagio's aesthetic. For example, a recurring motif of a spiral through the torso into a high release. To enhance 'Clarity of Form', I would practise this movement in front of a mirror, focusing specifically on the sequential articulation of the spine, ensuring each vertebra contributes to the spiral. I would film myself to analyse the line of my arms, ensuring they extend from the back and are not just 'placed'. I would also work on the transition out of the high release, focusing on maintaining control and avoiding a collapse. By breaking down the movement into its anatomical components and rehearsing for precision, I am moving beyond imitation and towards a deep, physical understanding that results in clear, intentional form, which is rewarded in the higher markbands for 'Technical and Performance Skills'.

sections.content: Expression is what transforms movement into dance. It is the 'how' and 'why' behind the 'what'. If the choreographer's intention is 'a sense of struggle and eventual release', your performance must physically manifest this journey. This is achieved not by pulling faces, but through the deliberate manipulation of performance qualities.

items: **Dynamic Range:** The most powerful tool for expression. Explore contrasts. A sharp, percussive gesture can be followed by a melting, sustained fall. This creates texture and interest, demonstrating a sophisticated understanding of the work's emotional landscape.

**Focus:** Your eyes are a potent communicative tool. An internal, soft focus can convey introspection or sadness. A sharp, direct focus can communicate confrontation or certainty. A shifting focus can indicate confusion or searching.

**Phrasing and Musicality:** Don't just dance *to* the music; dance *with* it. How does your breath connect to the musical phrases? Do you choose to accent a movement against the beat to create tension, or flow with the melody to create harmony? This demonstrates a deep interpretive skill.

**Projection:** This is the generous offering of your performance to the space and the audience. It is a commitment of energy that ensures even the smallest gesture is imbued with meaning and is visible and felt from a distance.

sections.content: In your performance, remember that stillness is as powerful as movement. A controlled, intentional pause can be more expressive than a flurry of steps. Use stillness to build tension, to shift focus, or to allow a moment to resonate. Examiners recognise this as a mature and confident performance choice.
3. The Symbiosis: Technique in Service of Expression

The highest achievement in performance is when technique and expression are inseparable. A technically weak dancer cannot fully realise their expressive intentions. For example, if you lack the core control to sustain a slow movement, you cannot express sustained longing; the physical struggle will override the intended emotion. Conversely, a technically proficient dancer who neglects expression is merely an acrobat. The goal is embodiment, where the physical execution is a direct and transparent conduit for the artistic idea.

sections.question: Reflect on a performance of a group piece where the artistic intention was 'mechanistic unity'. How did you use both technique and expression to communicate this?
sections.solution: To embody 'mechanistic unity' in our group performance, our technical focus was on absolute precision in timing and spatial formation. We drilled unison phrases relentlessly, using a metronome to ensure rhythmic accuracy. This technical precision was the foundation for our expression. Our expressive choice was to adopt a non-human, detached quality. This was achieved by restricting our dynamic range to primarily sharp, accented movements and maintaining a neutral facial expression with a fixed, forward focus. The technique (unison) and expression (detachment) worked together; any break in unison would have shattered the mechanistic illusion. My personal challenge was to maintain this expressive quality even when technically fatigued, ensuring my projection remained consistent and my movements retained their clipped, precise quality. This demonstrates the 'sustained' and 'compelling' communication of artistic intent required for the top assessment bands.

sections.content: The final stage is honing your performance for the formal assessment. This involves building stamina, developing performance confidence, and understanding how to present yourself and the work professionally. The criterion 'Presentation and Communication' evaluates the overall impact of your performance, including your engagement with the audience and the space.

items: **Rehearse Full Runs:** Practise performing the entire dance (or programme of dances) without stopping. This builds physical and mental stamina and prepares you for the reality of the assessment.

**Practise Recovery:** Mistakes can happen. A top-tier performer is one who can recover from a stumble or a memory lapse so seamlessly that the audience may not even notice. Practise improvising your way back into the choreography.
**Spatial Awareness:** Be fully aware of the performance space. Use your entrances and exits as part of the performance. Project your energy to all corners of the room.

**Self-Evaluation:** Film your rehearsals and watch them back, not as yourself, but as an examiner. Use the IB performance criteria as a checklist. Where is the 'clarity of form' lacking? Is the 'dynamic range' varied and appropriate to the intention? This critical analysis is vital for improvement.
